cu_txt_unit_wysiwyg.cs_txt_unit_wysiwyg: Sometimes, in order to a prove an arithmetical fact ∀xφ(x) by induction, straightforward induction “does not work”. Instead one “must” use a “stronger” induction hypothesis ψ(x) and prove ∀xψ(x), from which ∀xφ(x) may be derived. This is sometimes called “proof by strengthened induction hypothesis”. We show that this is an instance of a more general phenomenon which we call
. Cruacially, we show that there are cases where straightforward induction fails, but where proof by

Sometimes, in order to a prove an arithmetical fact ∀xφ(x) by induction, straightforward induction “does not work”. Instead one “must” use a “stronger” induction hypothesis ψ(x) and prove ∀xψ(x), from which ∀xφ(x) may be derived. This is sometimes called “proof by strengthened induction hypothesis”.
To give an example, suppose we want to prove that, for all natural numbers n, the sum of the first n odd numbers is a perfect square. Straightforward induction yields the following:
Base case: the sum of the first 0 odd numbers is 0, which is a perfect square.
Inductive step: if the sum of the first n odd numbers is a perfect square k², then the sum of the first n + 1 odd numbers is
But it is not true that k² + 2n + 1 is a perfect square for all k and n. So we are “stuck”.
Instead, we may prove the following
result by induction: for all natural numbers n, the sum of the first n odd numbers is n². Straightforward induction yields the following:
Base case: the sum of the 0 first odd numbers is 0, which is 0².
Inductive step: if the sum of the first n odd numbers is n², then the sum of the first n + 1 odd numbers is n² + 2n + 1 = (n + 1)².
What does it mean, more precisely, to say that straightforward induction to prove ∀xφ(x) “does not work”, and that we “must” use a “stronger” induction hypothesis and first prove ∀xψ(x)?
to characterize the situation (where PA is Peano arithmetic):
In fact, this situation is impossible:
yields PA ⊢ ∀xφ(x), which by pure logic yields

Instead, the situation may perhaps be characterized as follows: starting from the axioms of Peano arithmetic minus the induction axioms, we successively prove more and more theorems using logic and the rule of induction:
Suppose that, at some stage in this process of mathematical inquiry, we have reached a theory T consisting of the axioms and hitherto proved theorems. Then, as we show in
below, a situation may indeed arise satisfying the following definition:
We say that a theory T and formulas φ(x) and ψ(x) exemplify a case of
There are extensions T of Robinson arithmetic and formulas φ(x) and ψ(x) exemplifying cases of proof by non-straightforward induction.
Perhaps more surprisingly, proof by non-straightforward induction does not really require ψ(x) to be
than φ(x). As we show in
below, there are cases of proof by non-straightforward induction where, relative to T, ψ(x) is actually
We define a non-standard model M of Robinson arithmetic as follows. Let
be a countably infinite set disjoint from the natural numbers ℕ, and let the domain of the model be ℕ ∪ A. We shall call A the (set of)
(of M). Let the constant 0 be interpreted as the number 0, and extend the interpretation of the function symbols ', +, and × to the non-standard numbers as follows:
a[z] + n = n + a[z] = a[z+n] for z ∈ ℤ and n ∈ ℕ.
a[z] + a[u] = a[u] + a[z] = a[z+u] for z,u ∈ ℤ.
a[z] × n = n × a[z] = a[z × n] for z ∈ ℤ and n ∈ ℕ − {0}.
a[z] × a[u] = a[u] × a[z] = a[z × u] for z,u ∈ ℤ.
It can easily be verified that M is also a model of what we may call
, which is the theory you get by adding commutativity, associativity and distribution laws for addition and multiplication to the axioms of Robinson arithmetic.
To verify that cases of proof by non-straightforward induction are indeed possible, it suffices to find
in the sense of being satisfied by all natural numbers in the standard model as well as in the non-standard model) with the following profile regarding the non-standard numbers of M:
φ(x) | ... false false true false false ...
ψ(x) | ... false false false false false ...
The induction step will then fail for φ(x) with a[0] assigned to x, but will not fail for ψ(x). Granted as much, we can let T be the theory you get by adding, to minimal arithmetic, the axioms
will be true in M, the model will satisfy T. Moreover,
will be satisfied as witnessed by a[0] assigned to x.
For instance, let φ(x) be the formula
∀y∀z(x = x × x ∨ (y + x = z + x → y = z))
and let ψ(x) be the formula
Clearly, ψ(x) characterizes the non-standard numbers of M in the sense that they and only they fail to satisfy it. But due to
, φ(x) is satisfied by M in the special case of a[0] being assigned to x.
than φ(x), even in the sense that ⊢ ∀x(ψ(x) → φ(x)). Thus, this was a clear case of
really require a stronger induction hypothesis? No. As a matter of fact, there are cases of what we call
We say that a theory T and formulas φ(x) and ψ(x) exemplify a case of
just in case they exemplify a case of proof by non-straightforward induction and, in addition,
, we may still say that φ(x) and ψ(x) are
There are extensions T of Robinson arithmetic and formulas φ(x) and ψ(x) exemplifying a case of proof by incomparable induction hypothesis.
To show that proof by incomparable induction hypothesis is possible, it suffices to find true formulas φ(x) and ψ(x) with the following profile regarding the non-standard numbers of M:
φ(x) | ... true true true false false ...
ψ(x) | ... false false true true true ...
For instance, let φ(x) be the formula
∀y(x ≠ y ∧ ∃z(y + z = x) → x × x ≠ y × y)
and let ψ(x) be the formula
∀y(x ≠ y ∧ ∃z(x + z = y) → x × x ≠ y × y)
Observe that, by simple relabeling of variables, we have
Thus, let T be the theory you get by adding, to minimal arithmetic, the axiom
Clearly, both φ(x) and ψ(x) are true of all natural numbers in the standard model. To verify that φ(x) has the required profile with respect to M, we need to consider the following two cases:
x is assigned a natural number n. Whatever y is assigned that is distinct from n, it will be true in M that x × x ≠ y × y. So φ(x) is true of the natural numbers of M.

x is assigned a non-standard number a[i] for some integer i. Then either y is assigned a natural number, in which case M satisfies the antecedent x × x ≠ y × y, or y is assigned a non-standard number a[j] for some integer j ≠ i. By

, whenever j < i < 1, M satisfies x × x ≠ y × y. Likewise, whenever i > 0 and j = -i, M satisfies ∃z(y + z = x) and x × x = y × y. Thus, φ(x) is true in M for every non-standard number a[i] such that i < 1, and false whenever i > 0.
By symmetry of reasoning, we see that ψ(x) also has the required profile.
We say that a theory T and formulas φ(x) and ψ(x) exemplify a case of
just in case they they exemplify a case of proof by non-straightforward induction and, in addition,
, we may still say that ψ(x) is
There are extensions T of Robinson arithmetic and formulas φ(x) and ψ(x) exemplifying a case of proof by weakened induction hypothesis.
To show that proof by weakened induction hypothesis is possible, it suffices to find true formulas φ(x) and ψ(x) with the following profile:
φ(x) | ... false false true false false ...
ψ(x) | ... false false true true true ...
For instance, let φ(x) be the formula
and let ψ(x) be the formula
∀y(x ≠ y ∧ ∃z(x + z = y) → x × x ≠ y × y)
Observe that ⊢ ∀x(φ(x) → ψ(x)). It should be clear from
that the formulas have the required profile. Moreover, with u < v defined as
∀x∀y(x ≠ y ∧ ∃z(x + z = y) → x × x ≠ y × y)
, we have x < y or y < x. In the case of x < y, we have by definition that ∃z(x + z = y). Thus, by
, we have x × x ≠ y × y, as desired. In the case of y < x, we have by definition that ∃z(y + z = x). By
, we get by relabeling of variables that
∀y∀x(y ≠ x ∧ ∃z(y + z = x) → y × y ≠ x × x)
, we get y × y ≠ x × x, which yields the desired result by pure logic.
Lastly, we observe that M satisfies
. Thus, let T be the theory you get by adding, to minimal arithmetic, the axioms
We here have cases where, relative to a base theory T, one
use a different induction hypothesis, and
use one that is not stronger. Are there cases where one both
use a non-strengthened induction hypothesis? Indeed there are! (
). This follows from a result reported by Hetzl and Wong in
Proof by necessarily non-strengthened induction hypothesis

It can be shown that, if something is provable in an arithmetical theory containing all the induction axioms, it is provable in that theory using at most one induction axiom. This generalizes Corollary 2.10 reported by Hetzl and Wong.
For each formula Θ(x,z̄), we define the corresponding induction axiom IΘ as the closed formula
Generalizing Lemma 2.8 of Hetzl and Wong
Let Θ(x,z̄) be a formula, and define φ(x) as
. We have φ(0) by ∧-elimination, →-introduction and ∀-introduction. To show the second conjunct, assume φ(a), i.e.
, →-elimination yields Θ(a,b̄). By the second conjunct of
, →-introduction yields ⊢ φ(a) → φ(a'), and ∀-introduction finally yields ⊢ ∀x(φ(x) → φ(x')).
. For right to left, assume Iθ, i.e.
, →-elimination yields ∀xθ(x,b̄), which by ∀-elimination yields θ(a,b̄). Discharging
and →-elimination, this yields get θ(a,b̄). By ∀-introduction, this yields ∀xΘ(x,b̄). Discharging
Generalizing Proposition 2.9 of Hetzl and Wong
For any formulas θ₁(x,z̄), ..., θₙ(x,z̄), there is a formula φ(x) such that
, there is a formula φₖ(x) for each 1 ≤ k ≤ n such that
Define φ(x) as φ₁(x) ∧ ... ∧ φₙ(x). Then
For any formulas θ₁(x,z̄), ..., θₙ(x,z̄), there is a formula φ(x) such that ⊢ Iφ ↔ Iθ₁ ∧ ... ∧ Iθₙ.
, there is a formula φ(x) such that
What remains is to observe that ⊢ ∀xφ(x) ↔ Iφ. Left to right follows essentially by ∀-elimination. For right to left, assume Iφ. Then ∀xφ(x) follows by →-elimination.
Generalizing Corollary 2.10 of Hetzl and Wong
Let IND be the set of induction axioms, let T be a set of sentences, and let σ be a sentence. If T + IND ⊢ σ, then there is a formula φ(x) such that T + Iφ ⊢ σ.
Assume that T + IND ⊢ σ. Then there is a finite set θ₁(x,z̄), ..., θₙ(x,z̄) of formulas such that T + Iθ₁ + ... + Iθₙ ⊢ σ. By
, there is a formula φ(x) such that ⊢ Iφ ↔ Iθ₁ ∧ ... ∧ Iθₙ. Hence, T + Iφ ⊢ σ.
Generalizing Proposition 3.2 of Hetzl and Wong
Assume that T + IND ⊢ σ but T ⊬ σ, and let φ(x) be x = 0 → σ. Then we have
and there is ψ(x) such that
Moreover, for any such ψ(x), we have
. For the last part, assume that ψ(x) is a formula of the required kind. Since T ⊬ σ, there is a model M of T such that M ⊭ σ and thus M ⊭ ∀xφ(x). Hence, by
, we have M ⊭ Iψ, which means that M ⊨ ψ(0) but M ⊭ φ(0). Hence, M ⊭ ψ(0) → ϕ(0), so T ⊬ ∀x(ψ(x) → φ(x)).
The authors are not aware of any “natural” cases of proof by incomparable or weakened induction hypothesis. Observe that
does not yield a case where straightforward induction fails in the induction step, since T ⊬ ϕ(0) but T ⊢ ∀x(ϕ(x) → ϕ(x')).
Hetzl, S. and Wong, T. L. (2018). Some observations on the logical foundations of inductive theorem proving. Logical Methods in Computer Science, Volume 13, Issue 4.
